elements.sent-2411-001: In this work, we introduce a new class of problems in the study of (quantum) critical phenomena, termed ``deep boundary criticality''.
elements.sent-2411-002: Traditionally, critical systems are analyzed with two types of perturbations: those uniformly distributed throughout the bulk, which can significantly alter the bulk criticality by triggering a nontrivial bulk renormalization group flow, and those confined to a boundary or subdimensional defect, which affect only the boundary or defect condition.
elements.sent-2411-003: Here, we go beyond this paradigm by studying quantum critical systems with boundary perturbations that decay algebraically (following a power law) into the bulk.
elements.sent-2411-004: By continuously varying the decay exponent, such perturbations can transition between having no effect on the bulk and strongly influencing bulk behavior.
elements.sent-2411-005: We investigate this new regime using two prototypical models based on (1+1)D massless Dirac fermions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Through a combination of analytical and numerical approaches, we uncover exotic scaling laws in simple observables and observe qualitative changes in model behavior as the decay exponent varies.
elements.sent-2411-007: Quantum and classical critical phenomena, which can arise from continuous phase transitions and other contexts, are of fundamental importance in condensed matter and high-energy physics \cite{SachdevQPTBook,CFTBook .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In traditional studies of critical systems and nearby phases, two types of perturbations are typically considered: those uniformly distributed throughout the bulk and those supported on a boundary or subdimensional defect.
elements.sent-2411-009: The first type can significantly affect bulk properties by triggering a nontrivial bulk renormalization group (RG) flow.
elements.sent-2411-010: For instance, even a tiny magnetic field added to the critical Ising model disrupts the long-range correlations, effectively polarizing all spins at low energies.
elements.sent-2411-011: In contrast, the second type of perturbations is not expected to alter the bulk criticality \footnote{We are not aware of a general proof of this claim, but at least for a conformal field theory with a conformally invariant boundary/defect condition, boundary/defect perturbations do not induce any bulk RG flow to all orders in perturbation theory.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The study of boundary and defect conditions in critical systems, known as boundary or defect criticality, has a long history \cite{CardyBook,Diehl1997Review , with the famous Kondo problem serving as a classic example \cite{AffleckKondoReview .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This area has attracted renewed attention recently, partly due to connections with gapless symmetry-protected topological (gapless SPT) states.
elements.sent-2411-014: For early developments on this latter topic, see Refs.\,\onlinecite{Grover2012gSPT,Zhang2017gSPT2d,Scaffidi2017gSPT,Parker2018TopLL,Ding2018gSPT2d,Keselman2018GaplessTopSC,Weber2018gSPT2d,Verresen2021gSPT,Ji2020ConformalManifold,Xu2020gSPT3d,Verresen2020TITransitions,Jian2021gSPT2d,Hu2021RandgSPT,Thorngren2021igSPT .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Other significant recent advances include proofs \cite{Friedan2004gThm,Casini2016gThm and generalizations \cite{Cuomo2022DefectgThm,Casini2023DefectgThm of the $g$-theorem \cite{AffleckLudwiggThm on boundary and defect RG flows, the introduction of a new analytic bootstrap approach \cite{Kaviraj2018AnalyticBootstrapBCFT,Zhou2019AnalyticBootstrapBCFT , the discovery of the extraordinary-log boundary/defect universality class \cite{Metlitski2020ExtraordinaryLog,ParisenToldin2021ExtraordLog,Hu2021ExtraordLog,Metlitski2023ONModelDefect , and applications of the fuzzy-sphere numerical method \cite{He2023FuzzySphere,Zhu2024DefectFuzzySphere,Zou2024DefectFuzzySphere , a technique with broader relevance.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The list is by no means complete.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this work, we go beyond conventional settings to study a new type of critical phenomenon, which we dub deep boundary criticality.
elements.sent-2411-018: For concreteness, suppose we have a (1+1)D conformal field theory (CFT) defined on the half-infinite spatial domain $x \geq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-019: We deform the theory by adding the following term to the Lagrangian density: \begin{equation}{\lambda O(\tau,x)}/{x^\alpha},\end{equation} where $O(\tau,x)$ is a local operator and $\tau\in\mathbb{R}$ represents imaginary time.
elements.sent-2411-020: This perturbation decays algebraically from the boundary into the bulk, controlled by the parameter $\alpha > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-021: When $\alpha$ is large, so that the perturbation is strongly confined to the boundary, we expect a boundary CFT problem where the bulk criticality remains unaffected.
elements.sent-2411-022: In contrast, for small $\alpha$, particularly in the $\alpha \rightarrow 0$ limit, the perturbation can exert a nontrivial influence on the bulk, provided that $O$ has a sufficiently small scaling dimension $\Delta_{O}$.
elements.sent-2411-023: Power counting suggests that the critical decay exponent separating these scenarios is $\alpha_* = 2-\Delta_{O}$.
elements.sent-2411-024: This setup allows us to explore the universal physics that arises when $\alpha<\alpha_*$, $\alpha > \alpha_*$, and $\alpha = \alpha_*$.
elements.sent-2411-025: As a remark, deep boundary perturbations consist of strictly local terms and are distinct from long-range interactions, though they may appear similar.
elements.sent-2411-026: We restrict our attention to two simple models based on the (1+1)D Dirac fermion CFT.
elements.sent-2411-027: Although these are free fermion models, they exhibit surprisingly nontrivial behavior while offering both analytical and numerical tractability.
elements.sent-2411-028: We uncover exotic scaling laws in simple observables and indeed observe qualitative changes in model behavior as $\alpha$ varies through $\alpha_*$.
elements.sent-2411-029: A more detailed summary of our results is given in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-030: We focus on the (1+1)D massless Dirac fermion field theory with a two-component spinor $\psi:=(\psi_+,\psi_-)^{T}$, placed on the half-infinite space $x\geq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-031: Without loss of generality, we impose the boundary condition $\psi_+ + \psi_- =0$ at $x=0$.
elements.sent-2411-032: A more general boundary condition is $\psi_+ + e^{i\gamma}\psi_- =0$, but we can always utilize the chiral U(1) symmetry to set $\gamma=0$.
elements.sent-2411-033: We then add the following deep boundary perturbation to the Hamiltonian \begin{equation}\Delta H=\int_\delta^\infty dx~\frac{\lambda}{x^\alpha}m(x),\end{equation} where $\lambda$ is a coupling constant, $\alpha>0$ controls the decaying of the perturbation, $m(x)$ is a bulk local operator to be specified, and $\delta>0$ is a short-distance cutoff.
elements.sent-2411-034: We choose $m(x)$ to be either of the following two mass operators: \begin{equation}m_Y:=\psi^\da\sigma_y\psi,\quad m_X:=\psi^\da\sigma_x\psi.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-035: This produces two models: If $m=m_Y$ ($m=m_X$), the theory will be called Model Y (Model X).
elements.sent-2411-036: We note that $m_Y$ and $m_X$ have different symmetry properties.
elements.sent-2411-037: The unperturbed Dirac fermion theory with the chosen boundary condition enjoys a unitary particle-hole symmetry: $\psi\leftrightarrow\psi^\dagger$.
elements.sent-2411-038: While $m_Y$ preserves this symmetry, $m_X$ is antisymmetric.
elements.sent-2411-039: We are interested in universal properties of these two models for different values of $\alpha$ and $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-040: On general grounds, given $\Delta_m=1$ in both models, we expect qualitatively different behaviors for $\alpha>1$ and $\alpha<1$.
elements.sent-2411-041: Moreover, $\alpha=1$ with arbitrary $\lambda\neq 0$ marks new renormalization group (RG) fixed points that were not previously studied.
elements.sent-2411-042: Our main focus is the conceptually most straightforward observable: vacuum expectation value of $m(x)$ in the thermodynamic limit (infinite system size).
elements.sent-2411-043: Just a reminder, $m$ has different meanings in the two models.
elements.sent-2411-044: By combining numerical exact diagonalization for large systems and analytical solutions, we are able to determine the leading asymptote of $\ex{m(x)}$ at large $x$ for all $\alpha$ and $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-045: In particular, the magnitude of $\lambda$ need not be small.
elements.sent-2411-046: The results are listed in Table~\ref{tab:LeadingAsymptote , where we can clearly see the distinction between $\alpha<1$ and $\alpha>1$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Note that when $\alpha=1$, $\ex{m(x)}$ does not scale as $1/x$ which one may na\"{i vely conclude from scale invariance.}

elements.sent-2411-048: For the special value $\alpha=1$, we also obtain very interesting results about higher order corrections.
elements.sent-2411-049: Define \begin{equation}\beta:= |2\lambda-1| for Model Y |2\lambda| for Model X ,\end{equation} and focusing on the regime $0<\beta<1$, we find the following contribution in the mass expectation value. \begin{equation}\boxed{ \alpha=1:\quad\ex{m(x)}\supset \frac{\rm const}{x^{1+\beta}} }\end{equation} Moreover, this is the most significant component other than the $\log(x)/x$-scaling and $1/x$-scaling terms.

elements.sent-2411-051: The rest of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-052: In Section~\ref{sec:ModelY , we present detailed analytical analysis and numerical results for Model Y, including a discussion about topological edge state not summarized above.}
elements.sent-2411-053: In Section~\ref{sec:ModelX , we elaborate on results about Model X, with an emphasis on its difference from Model Y.}
elements.sent-2411-054: We conclude and comment on future directions in Section~\ref{sec:Conclusion .}

elements.section-2411-003: Model Y: Dirac Fermions with a Particle-Hole Symmetric Perturbation
elements.sent-2411-056: The unperturbed theory that we will consider is the (1+1)D massless Dirac fermion theory described by the following Euclidean Lagrangian density. \begin{equation}\mc L_0=\psi^\da_+(\pa_\tau+i\pa_x)\psi_+ + \psi^\da_-(\pa_\tau-i\pa_x)\psi_-, \label{eq:UnperturbedDirac}\end{equation} where $\psi_\pm(\tau,x)$ is the left/right-moving complex fermion field.
elements.sent-2411-057: We place the theory on the half-infinite space manifold $x\geq 0$, and impose the boundary condition \begin{equation}\psi_+(\tau,0)+\psi_-(\tau,0)=0. \label{eq:DiracBdryCondition}\end{equation} The Lagrangian density \eqref{eq:UnperturbedDirac and the boundary condition \eqref{eq:DiracBdryCondition both preserve the particle-hole symmetry $\psi_\pm\leftrightarrow \psi_\pm^\dagger$.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Correlation functions in this free fermion theory can be computed using the doubling trick.
elements.sent-2411-059: For all $x\geq 0$, define $\psi_+(\tau,-x)=-\psi_-(\tau,x)$ which respects the boundary condition \eqref{eq:DiracBdryCondition .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Then, we can map the original nonchiral theory with a spatial boundary to a chiral theory without spatial boundary: \begin{equation}S_0 =\int_{x\geq 0}d\tau dx~\mc L_0=\int_{x\inR} d\tau dx~ \psi^\da_+(\pa_\tau+i\pa_x)\psi_+.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-061: The fermion two-point correlation function in this chiral theory can be obtained from the Schwinger-Dyson equation, and takes the following form. \begin{equation}\ex{\psi_+(z_1)\psi^\da_+(z_2)}=\frac{1}{2\pi (z_1-z_2)},\end{equation} where the complex coordinate $z=\tau+ix$ has been used and $\psi_+(z_i)\equiv\psi_+(\tau_i,x_i)$.
elements.sent-2411-062: The field theory \eqref{eq:UnperturbedDirac with the boundary condition \eqref{eq:DiracBdryCondition has a simple lattice regularization which enables numerical simulations.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: This lattice theory lives on a half-infinite chain with sites labeled by $j=1,2,3,\cdots$.
elements.sent-2411-064: There is a single flavor of complex fermions hopping on this chain, described by the following Hamiltonian. \begin{equation}H_{{\rm latt},0}=t\sum_{j=1}^\infty (c_j^\da c_{j+1}+h.c.), \label{eq:LatticeDiracHamiltonian}\end{equation} where $t>0$ and $c_j$ is the fermion annihilation operator on site $j$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Let $a$ be the lattice constant, or equivalently, the spatial coordinate of the $j$-th site be $x_j=ja$.
elements.sent-2411-066: We take $t=1/(2a)$ so that the fermi velocity equals to $1$.
elements.sent-2411-067: The lattice and continuum fermion operators can be identified as follows. \begin{equation}\frac{c_{x/a}}{\sqrt{a}}\simeq e^{ik_F x}\psi_+(x)+e^{-ik_F x}\psi_-(x), \label{eq:LattContOpCorresp}\end{equation} where $k_F=\pi/(2a)$ is the fermi momentum.
elements.sent-2411-068: For completeness, we derive this lattice-continuum correspondence in Appendix \ref{app:LatticeDiracFermions .}
elements.sent-2411-069: We observe that this lattice model also has an exact particle-hole symmetry $c_j\leftrightarrow(-1)^j c_j^\dagger$ which corresponds to the aforementioned particle-hole symmetry in the continuum.
elements.sent-2411-070: The deep boundary perturbation that we will study in this section takes the following form. \begin{equation}\Delta\mc L=\frac{\lambda}{x^\alpha}m_Y(\tau,x),\quad m_Y:=-i(\psi^\da_+\psi_- - \psi^\da_-\psi_+). \label{eq:DeepmYperturbation}\end{equation} We may cut off $\Delta\mc L$ at a small distance $\delta$ near the boundary, so that the total action takes the form \begin{equation}S=\int_{x\geq 0} d\tau dx~\mc L_0 +\int_{x\geq \delta}d\tau dx~\Delta\mc L.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-071: This is referred to as Model Y throughout this paper.
elements.sent-2411-072: Note that $m_Y$ is a mass operator for the Dirac fermions; that is, the fermions will become massive if $m_Y$ is uniformly added to the Lagrangian.
elements.sent-2411-073: An alternative mass operator is $m_X:=\psi^\da_+\psi_- + \psi^\da_-\psi_+$, and in the next section, we will discuss the effect of a deep boundary $m_X$ perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-075: The lattice counterpart of $m_Y$ is as follows.. \begin{equation}m_Y(x)\simeq \frac{M_{Y,x/a}}{a}, M_{Y,j}:=\frac{1}{2}(\tilde M_{Y,j-1/2}+\tilde M_{Y,j+1/2}), \tilde M_{Y,j+1/2}:=\frac{1}{2}(-1)^j\left(c^\da_j c_{j+1}+c^\da_{j+1}c_j \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-077: This correspondence enables us to numerically simulate the perturbed theory.
elements.sent-2411-078: More precisely, we can add the following term to the unperturbed lattice Hamiltonian in \eqref{eq:LatticeDiracHamiltonian .. \begin{equation}\Delta H_{\rm latt}=\sum_{j=2}^\infty \frac{\lambda a^{1-\alpha}}{j^\alpha}M_{Y,j}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Here, the sum over $j$ starts from $j=2$ because $M_{Y,j}$ is only well-defined for $j\geq 2$.
elements.sent-2411-080: In actual numerical computations, we need to consider a finite lattice with $N$ sites, the $j$ sum is then also constrained by the upper bound $j\leq N-1$.
elements.sent-2411-081: All numerical results presented in this paper have $N=2\times 10^4$.
elements.sent-2411-083: Therefore, just by na\"{i ve power counting, we expect the perturbation to be relevant (irrelevant) for the bulk criticality when $\alpha<1$ ($\alpha>1$), and $\alpha=1$ is the marginal case.}
elements.sent-2411-084: This is reflected in the energy spectrum of the system on a finite space.
elements.sent-2411-085: Let $L$ be the length of the space.
elements.sent-2411-086: In the absence of any perturbation, the finite-size energy gap is of the order $1/L$.
elements.sent-2411-087: After we turn on the perturbation, the Dirac fermions acquire a spatially varying mass that is everywhere no less than $\lambda/L^\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Hence, if $\alpha<1$, there should be an energy gap of the order $\lambda/L^\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-089: On the contrary, if $\alpha\geq 1$, we expect the energy gap to be still of the order $1/L$.
elements.sent-2411-090: We have verified these claims numerically.
elements.sent-2411-091: In the rest of this section, we investigate the effect of the deep boundary perturbation on the mass expectation value $\ex{m_Y(x)}$.
elements.sent-2411-092: With the deep boundary perturbation in \eqref{eq:DeepmYperturbation , the asymptotic form of $\ex{m_Y(x)}$ for large $x$ is found to be \begin{equation}\ex{m_Y(x)}\sim  \displaystyle -\frac{\alpha\lambda\log x}{\pi x^\alpha} (\alpha\leq 1) \displaystyle\frac{1}{2\pi x} (\alpha>1) . \label{eq:mYVevLeading}\end{equation} Note that the symbol $\sim$ is used here to denote the leading term in an asymptotic expansion with its precise multiplicative factor.}
elements.sent-2411-093: We obtain this result by combining numerical simulations and analytical solutions.
elements.sent-2411-094: The numerical results are given in Fig.\,\ref{fig:YMassVev , matching very well with the above expressions.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Our analytical approaches will be elaborated below in this subsection.
elements.sent-2411-096: We would like to emphasize that, although the asymptotic expressions presented above are at most linear in $\lambda$, they are supposed to be nonperturbative.
elements.sent-2411-097: In other words, for any fixed $\lambda$ that is not necessarily small, these expressions should correctly capture the leading behavior of $\ex{m_Y(x)}$ as $x\rightarrow\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-098: Our numerical simulations are also not restricted to small values of $\lambda$.

elements.sent-2411-104: We will take an RG approach, which is nothing but a scale transformation for a free fermion field theory.
elements.sent-2411-105: We write the action as $S=S_0+\Delta S$ where $S_0$ is the unperturbed theory and \begin{equation}\Delta S=\int_{x\geq\delta} d\tau dx~\frac{\lambda}{x^\alpha}m(\tau,x).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-108: To implement a scale transformation, we define $x'=x/b$, $\tau'=\tau/b$, $\psi_\pm'(\tau',x')=b^{1/2}\psi_{\pm (\tau,x)$ for some constant $b>1$.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Rewrite $S$ in terms of the primed coordinates and fields, and finally remove the primes by renaming, we find that $S_0$ is invariant, while $\Delta S$ changes to \begin{equation}\Delta S'=\int_{x\geq \delta/b}d\tau dx~\frac{\lambda'}{x^\alpha} m(\tau,x),\end{equation} where $\lambda'=\lambda b^{1-\alpha}$.

elements.section-2411-004: Model X: Dirac Fermions with a Particle-Hole Antisymmetric Perturbation
elements.sent-2411-209: In this section, we will consider another deep boundary perturbation: \begin{equation}\Delta\mc L=\frac{\lambda}{x^\alpha}m_X(\tau,x),\quad m_X:=\psi^\da_+\psi_- +\psi^\da_-\psi_+. \label{eq:DeepmXperturbation}\end{equation} Again, we will cut off $\Delta\mc L$ at a small distance $\delta$ near the boundary, i.e. the change of the action is $\int_{x\geq\delta d\tau dx~\Delta\mc L$.}
elements.sent-2411-210: This is referred to as Model X throughout this paper.
elements.sent-2411-211: As we mentioned previously, $m_X$ is antisymmetric under the particle-hole transformation, which in particular implies that $\ex{m_X(x) _0=0$ in the unperturbed theory.}
elements.sent-2411-212: The lattice counterpart of $m_X$ is \begin{equation}m_X(x)\simeq \frac{M_{X,x/a}}{a}, M_{X,j+1/2}:=\frac{1}{2}(\tilde M_{X,j}+\tilde M_{X,j+1}), \tilde M_{X,j}:=(-1)^j c^\da_j c_j. \label{eq:LatticemX}\end{equation} See Appendix~\ref{app:LatticeDiracFermions for details.}

elements.sent-2411-215: In the rest of this section, we explore the effect of the deep boundary perturbation on the mass expectation value $\ex{m_X(x)}$.

elements.sent-2411-253: In summary, we have investigated the effects of deep boundary perturbations in the (1+1)D massless Dirac fermion theory, uncovering nontrivial behaviors as the decay exponent and coupling constant vary.
elements.sent-2411-254: Through this study, we introduce a new class of problems within (quantum) critical phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-255: We hope that our findings will inspire further research and deepen our understanding of boundary effects in critical systems.
elements.sent-2411-256: We suggest a few possible directions for future exploration below.
elements.sent-2411-257: There are remaining interesting questions for the two models studied in this work.
elements.sent-2411-258: For example, how do more general correction functions behave? In particular, how do correctors of the form $\ex{O_1(\tau,x)O_2(0,x)}$ scale for large $\tau$? It is also natural to explore the properties of ground state entanglement entropy.
elements.sent-2411-259: One can analyze many other models with deep boundary/defect perturbations, especially interacting systems, such as (1+1)D Ising CFT perturbed by a deep boundary magnetic field that explicitly break the Ising $\mathbb{Z _2$ symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-260: Another interesting possibility is to turn on multiple deep boundary perturbations and study the interplay between them.

elements.sent-2411-265: In this appendix, we elaborate on the continuum limit of the lattice fermion model \eqref{eq:LatticeDiracHamiltonian , and show that it matches with the Dirac fermion theory \eqref{eq:UnperturbedDirac with the boundary condition \eqref{eq:DiracBdryCondition .}}}
elements.sent-2411-266: Before considering any boundary effect, let us first try to understand the continuum limit of the model on a closed chain with perodic boundary condition.
elements.sent-2411-267: Recall the Hamiltonian takes the following form, \begin{equation}H_{{\rm latt},0}=t\sum_{j} (c_j^\da c_{j+1}+h.c.)\quad (t>0). \label{eq:LatticeHamiltonianApp}\end{equation} In the case of periodic boundary condition, we identify $j$ with $j+N$ where $N$ is the total number of sites.
elements.sent-2411-268: This Hamiltonian can be easily diagonalized by Fourier transform: \begin{equation}c_k=\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sum_j e^{-ik x_j}c_j~ \Leftrightarrow~ c_j=\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sum_k e^{ik x_j} c_k,\end{equation} where $x_j=ja$, $a$ is the lattice constant, $k\in (\Delta k) \mathbb{Z}$, $\Delta k=2\pi/(Na)$, and $k$ is identified with $k+2\pi/a$.
elements.formula-2411-059: \begin{equation}c_k=\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sum_j e^{-ik x_j}c_j~ \Leftrightarrow~ c_j=\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\sum_k e^{ik x_j} c_k,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-269: Be warned that we are somewhat lazy about the notations: We simply use the subscripts ($j$ or $k$) to distinguish operators in the real and momentum spaces.
elements.sent-2411-270: In terms of the Fourier modes, \begin{equation}H_{{\rm latt},0}=2t\sum_k \cos(ka)c^\da_k c_k.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-271: We define the vacuum state as the (many-body) state with all negative-energy modes occupied.
elements.sent-2411-272: Given that $t>0$, the unoccupied modes are in the range $-k_F\leq k \leq k_F$, where $k_F=\pi/(2a)$.
elements.sent-2411-274: The continuum limit more precisely means the following: $N\rightarrow\infty$, $a\rightarrow 0$, $t\rightarrow\infty$, while fixing $L=Na$ that defines the length of the space manifold, and fixing $v_F=2ta$.
elements.sent-2411-275: In order to take this limit, we introduce fermion field operators $\psi_\pm(k)$ in the momentum space as follows: \begin{equation}c_{\pm k_F+k}\equiv \sqrt{\frac{\Delta k}{2\pi}}\psi_\pm(k)\quadfor\quad |k|<\Lambda\ll k_F.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-276: Note that a cutoff scale $\Lambda$ has been introduced.
elements.sent-2411-277: We will fix the value of $\Lambda$ when taking the continuum limit \footnote{One may further take the $\Lambda\rightarrow\infty$ limit on top of the continuum limit.}
elements.sent-2411-278: The normalization factor is chosen to reproduce the conventional anticommutation relations as $L\rightarrow\infty$ ($\Delta k=2\pi/L\rightarrow 0$), e.g. $\{ \psi_+(k_1),\psi^\da_+(k_2) \}=(2\pi/\Delta k)\delta_{k_1,k_2}\rightarrow 2\pi\delta(k_1-k_2)$.
elements.sent-2411-279: We further define the real-space fermion field operators $\psi_\pm(x)$ as follows: \begin{equation}\psi_\pm(x)=\sum_{k,|k|<\Lambda}\frac{\Delta k}{2\pi}e^{ikx}\psi_\pm(k)\overset{L\rightarrow\infty}{=}\int_{-\Lambda}^\Lambda\frac{dk}{2\pi}~e^{ikx}\psi_\pm(k),\end{equation} which implies, in the continuum limit, \begin{equation}\psi_\pm(k)=\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} dx~e^{-ikx}\psi_\pm(x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-280: We can now also establish the lattice-continuum correspondence between real-space operators.

elements.sent-2411-510: Given that the bare operator has no dependence on the renomalization scale $\mu$, we have \begin{equation}0=\frac{d}{d\log\mu}\log\ex{m_Y(x)} =\frac{d}{d\log\mu}\log[Z_m^{-1}\ex{m_{Y,R}(x)}] =2\lambda+\frac{d}{d\log\mu}\log\ex{m_{Y,R}(x)}+O(\lambda^2).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-511: Let us now integrate $\mu$ from the low-energy scale $x^{-1}$ all the way to $\Lambda$.. This gives \begin{equation}\log\left[ \frac{\ex{m_{Y,R}(x)}_{\Lambda}}{\ex{m_{Y,R}(x)}_{x^{-1}}} \right]=-2\lambda\log\left( \Lambda x \right)+O(\lambda^2).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-512: We know that $\ex{m_{Y,R (x) _{x^{-1 =w(\lambda,\Lambda x)/x$ for some dimensionless function $w$ that is finite in the $\Lambda\rightarrow \infty$ limit.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-513: Moreover, when $\mu$ is set to be $\Lambda$, the renormalization factor $Z_m$ evaluates to a dimensionless function of $\lambda$, denoted as $z(\lambda)$.
elements.sent-2411-515: If ignoring the higher order corrections, we have found that $\ex{m_Y(x)}\propto 1/x^{1+2\lambda}$ at large $x$, and this is inconsistent with our exact solution.
elements.sent-2411-516: Next, consider the first approach where the counter term is additive.. We have \begin{equation}0=\frac{d}{d\log\mu}\ex{m_Y(x)}=\frac{d}{d\log\mu}\ex{m_{Y,R}(x)}+\frac{\lambda}{\pi x}+O(\lambda^2).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-517: Again integrating over $\mu$, we obtain \begin{equation}z(\lambda) \ex{m_Y(x)}=\frac{w(\lambda,\Lambda x)}{x}-\frac{\lambda}{\pi x}\log(\Lambda x)+O(\lambda^2).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-518: The result has nothing new, but is indeed consistent with our exact solution.. \bibliographystyle{JHEP.}
